data.who_my_customer_is: A guardian (usually a parent) of a new or struggling 9th grader who watched the jump to high school hit all at once: more classes, more independence, and no system to hold it together. Note: Elena is a made-up example teen. These quotes are illustrative and are not real OTS customer data.

interviews.name: Parent 1 (pseudonym)
interviews.summary: Talked about the blank planner and the fear of finding out too late. Guardian Rosa was in the loop for the recording.

interviews.name: Student 1 (pseudonym)
interviews.summary: Said high school just expects you to already be organized. Recorded with the student's and their parent's permission; Rosa reviewed the notes.

quotes.verbatim_text: I lie awake worried he is already behind and I will not catch it until report cards.
The planner comes home blank every single day. I have no idea what is even due.
I just want one system she actually uses, not another app we forget about by October.
High school just expects them to be organized on day one, and nobody teaches that part.
Seventy-nine dollars is a lot for one session. How do I know it will stick after you leave?
For something that actually gets used, I would pay somewhere around eighty to a hundred dollars.

top_three: Guardians want ONE system that actually gets used, not more apps.
The blank planner and the missed-assignment surprise are the daily pain.
The real fear is finding out too late, at report-card time.

data.hero_composite: Rosa, a guardian of a nervous new 9th grader who was organized enough in middle school but is now drowning in the first weeks of high school. She is not looking for a tutor. She wants her kid to run one simple system without being nagged. (Made-up example hero, not real OTS data.)

external_problem.text: The planner sits empty, the backpack is a black hole, and assignments slip through in the first weeks of high school.

internal_problem.text: The guardian feels anxious and a little helpless, watching their kid start behind and not knowing how to help without nagging.

philosophical_problem.text: A 14-year-old should not have to invent a whole organization system alone in week one just because high school assumes they already have one.

data.guide: Elena has been the new 9th grader. She empathizes first (she remembers the blank planner), then shows authority: a simple, repeatable setup she has run start to finish. Organization coaching only.
data.plan: 1. Take the free Freshman Friction Point Quiz to name the biggest friction point. 2. Book one 75-minute LaunchPad Freshman Reset session with the student and guardian. 3. Run the one system for 14 days with a short follow-up sequence.
data.cta: Book the LaunchPad Freshman Reset session ($79). Free first step: take the Freshman Friction Point Quiz.
data.failure: The year drifts, the planner stays blank, missing work piles up, and the family finds out at report cards when it is late and stressful.
data.success: The student runs one simple school system on their own, the guardian stops nagging, and the semester starts organized instead of overwhelmed.
data.one_liner: Most families hit 9th grade and the middle-school routines quietly fall apart. I help a new or struggling 9th grader and their guardian set up one simple school system that actually gets used, so the year starts organized instead of overwhelmed.
dinner_party_tests: Said it to a neighbor: she asked 'so is it tutoring?' and I clarified it is organization coaching. Passed after one tweak.
Said it to an uncle: he immediately said 'my nephew needs that in September.' Passed.
data.voice_memo: Plain, calm, a little confessional. Short sentences. Words I use: system, friction, planner, week one. Words I ban: hack, guaranteed, straight-As, effortless. No hype, no grade promises.
story_file: My own 9th grade week one: three binders, zero system, and a planner I never opened. That is the story I keep coming back to.
A friend's little brother missed a whole project because it lived on a sticky note that fell off the fridge. The friction was invisible until it was a zero.

paid_offer.name: LaunchPad Freshman Reset
paid_offer.who_for: A new or struggling 9th grader and their guardian.
paid_offer.deliverables: One 75-minute setup session, a personalized weekly planner template, a backpack and Google Drive cleanup checklist, and a 14-day follow-up email sequence.
paid_offer.refund_promise_exact_words: Full refund within 14 days if it is not a fit.
paid_offer.payment_link: Set by Rosa in Stripe (example binder, no live checkout).

free_front_door.name: Freshman Friction Point Quiz
free_front_door.what: A short, free quiz that helps a guardian name their student's single biggest organization friction point in about two minutes.

claims_allowed.claim: One 75-minute live setup session with the student and their guardian.
claims_allowed.proof_source: Session length set in Elena's booking calendar and stated the same way on the sales page and terms page.

claims_allowed.claim: Full refund within 14 days if it is not a fit.
claims_allowed.proof_source: Refund promise written word for word on the terms page, reviewed by Rosa (guardian) before it went live.

claims_never: Any promise of better grades or a GPA result
Calling it tutoring or therapy (it is organization coaching only)
data.scarcity_check: Sessions are 1:1 and live, so only a real, small number fit in a week. Any 'spots left this week' number must match Elena's actual open calendar slots. No fake countdowns.

front_door_history.change: Rewrote the free quiz opener around 'Find your student's biggest friction point.'
front_door_history.reason: Week-1 scoreboard (example numbers, not real OTS data) showed parents clicked the quiz but left before giving their email because the opener did not promise a clear result.

sections.dream_outcome: In their words: "I just want one system she actually uses, not another app we forget about by October." A first semester where the student runs one simple routine on their own and the guardian can finally stop nagging.
sections.stack:
1. 75-minute live setup session (student + guardian). Honest value: $79, the price Elena charges for the session itself. Why: this is the core, the hands-on hour where the system gets built together.
2. Personalized weekly planner template. Honest value: about $15 (a fair comparable for a custom template). Why: gives the student the exact page to run every week.
3. Backpack and Google Drive cleanup checklist. Honest value: about $10 comparable. Why: clears the mess that hides missing work.
4. 14-day follow-up email sequence. Honest value: about $20 comparable. Why: makes the system stick past day one.
(Example values, not real OTS data. Total honest value about $124, offered at $79.)
sections.bonuses:
Bonus 1: A one-page 'How to support without nagging' guide for the guardian. Answers the objection 'How do I know it will stick after you leave?'
Bonus 2: A 10-minute midpoint check-in message during the 14 days. Answers the objection 'What if it stops getting used?'
sections.guarantee: Full refund within 14 days if it is not a fit. Organization coaching only. Not therapy, not tutoring. No promise of better grades.
